query: effectiveness of cabotegravir in HIV prevention

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Preexposure Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir can be used for the prevention of sexual transmission of HIV across populations, including those PWID who are also at risk of acquiring HIV through sex and pregnant people. [1] It is given as a gluteal intramuscular injection every 2 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Medication Adherence
retrieved_docs.content: While adherence to therapy may be improved with intramuscular cabotegravir due to the simple bimonthly dosing schedule, patients may experience barriers similar to those encountered with oral PrEP due to the need for ongoing connection with healthcare services. Medication adherence requires an interdisciplinary team approach that identifies and addresses all adherence barriers.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Complications -- Adverse Events
retrieved_docs.content: Long-acting cabotegravir and TDF/FTC likewise demonstrated similar risks of serious adverse events. There were no differences between long-acting cabotegravir and TDF/FTC in serious kidney or liver adverse events, discontinuation of therapy due to liver events, or sexually transmitted infections. [35] [64] Injection site reactions such as pain, tenderness, and induration were reported in 81.4% of patients receiving cabotegravir. Reactions were generally mild, disappeared within a few days, and occurred most frequently with the first 2 to 3 injections. These reactions can be managed with over-the-counter pain medications and hot compresses for 15 to 20 minutes after the injection. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Preexposure prophylaxis of HIV (PrEP) is an essential component of the recommended package for HIV prevention, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) and United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). [3] [4] Preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) involves the intake of antiretroviral medications either continuously or for identified high-risk events and is highly effective in preventing HIV. Compared to placebo, consistent use of daily oral PrEP reduces the risk of sexual transmission of HIV by up to 92%. [5] PrEP with bimonthly injectable cabotegravir lowers the chance of sexual transmission of HIV by about 70% compared to those taking tenofovir disiproxil fumarate/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC). [6] [CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Though one trial of PWID observed individuals 40 years or older, those who used methamphetamines, and those with recent incarcerations had lower adherence than those without these risk factors, other studies have indicated the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of PrEP in preventing HIV in this population. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV and AIDS -- Treatment / Management -- Integrase Strand Transfer Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Raltegravir Elvitegravir with boosting agent cobicistat Dolutegravir Bictegravir Cabotegravir

retrieved_docs.title: HIV-2 Infection -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) should be offered to patients at substantial risk of acquiring HIV infection. Although there are no data explicitly addressing PrEP in preventing HIV-2, the antiretrovirals used for PrEP are active against both HIV-1 and HIV-2, and similarly, high levels of efficacy would be expected. [58] Currently, less is known about the potential effectiveness of long-acting injectable PrEP in the prevention of HIV-2, though recent in vitro studies results have shown cabotegravir to have efficacy against HIV-2. [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Choice of Medication
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, given as a 600-mg gluteal intramuscular injection every 2 months, can be used for the prevention of sexual transmission of HIV across populations, including those who inject drugs and are also at risk of acquiring HIV through sex, pregnant people, and youth. [30] Further, it may be particularly appropriate for people with renal disease, with difficulty adhering to oral PrEP, or who prefer receiving injections every 2 months rather than taking daily oral medication.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Medication Adherence
retrieved_docs.content: Patients receiving cabotegravir injections must be advised that if they discontinue medication, the gradually declining levels of cabotegravir raise the risk of developing a drug-resistant strain if HIV infection occurs at that time.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Therefore, individuals need to keep regular injection appointments.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Improving Access to Quality HIV Prevention and Care Services
retrieved_docs.content: Putting patients at the center allows maximum benefit from TaSP, PrEP, and other prevention measures and improved clinical and public health outcomes.[WHO. People-Centred Health Services. 2016][WHO. Men and HIV Interventions. 2023].  For example, due to its ease of use for those facing barriers to regular, daily medication use, cabotegravir may be ideal for PrEP. To improve patient-centered care, the WHO recommends linking strategies and treatment of related diseases or syndromes, such as in its strategy for HIV, viral hepatitis, and STIs.[WHO. Global Health Sector HIV Strategies. 2022]  Important patient-centered outcomes such as self-image can be improved, and self-stigma can be reduced through interventions such as TasP, in addition to more standard outcomes such as increased testing, adherence to therapy, and viral suppression. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The use of antiretrovirals as PrEP is a less expensive, more straightforward, and more effective way to prevent HIV in higher-risk populations compared to treatment with antiretroviral therapy. Results from multiple large trials demonstrated the efficacy of oral PrEP in decreasing HIV transmission risk in people at high risk of acquiring HIV across sex, age, risk category, and study duration. [30] [31] [32] [33] The risk of HIV is up to 92% lower for people who take oral PrEP consistently compared to those who take a placebo. The effectiveness of PrEP is significantly reduced if used inconsistently.[CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021] The first long-acting injectable PrEP, cabotegravir, was approved in the United States in 2021. Men, women, and transgender women in the cabotegravir groups of 2 large studies had a 69% to 90% lower chance of contracting HIV through sexual transmission than those taking tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine. [34] [35] [36] [CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Choice of Medication
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir can be initiated with 30 mg of daily oral cabotegravir for 4 to 5 weeks before injection for those who have anxiety about adverse events, but it should not be prescribed as ongoing therapy. Oral initiation is not required as trials of cabotegravir have not demonstrated safety concerns.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Injection must be intragluteal as the pharmacokinetics in other administration sites are unknown.

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Telehealth or telephone check-ins, smartphone reminders, delivery services, directly observed therapy, and pillboxes may enhance adherence. Switching to a different formulation or regime for oral PrEP (eg, moving to on-demand) or switching to long-acting intramuscular cabotegravir may also improve adherence. Seeking further financial support may improve adherence for those who do not have insurance and cannot afford the medications. For patients with substance use disorders, referral for counseling or a treatment program may be successful. Options should be tailored to fit the client's preferences and life situation. All measures must be supportive, recognizing the challenges in maintaining long-term adherence to therapy and the socioeconomic context in which the client lives.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Raltegravir: None

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Ongoing Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: For patients on injectable cabotegravir, a negative nucleic acid amplification test is required 1 to 2 months after initiation of therapy and every 2 months before each injection. Regular sexually transmitted infection screening should also be performed every 6 months for heterosexually active women and men and every 4 months for MSM and transgender women.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021]

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Preexposure Prophylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple large trials demonstrate the efficacy of oral PrEP in decreasing HIV transmission risk in people at high risk of acquiring HIV across sex, age, risk category, and study duration. The risk of HIV is up to 92% lower for people who take oral PrEP consistently compared to those who take a placebo. The effectiveness of PrEP is significantly reduced with inconsistent use.[CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021] The first long-acting injectable PrEP, cabotegravir, was approved in the United States in 2021. Men, women, and transgender women in the cabotegravir arms of 2 large studies had a 69% to 90% lower chance of contracting HIV through sexual transmission than those taking tenofovir disiproxil fumarate (TDF)/emtricitabine.[CDC. PrEP HIV Prevention in the US. 2021]

retrieved_docs.title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Choice of Medication
retrieved_docs.content: Three PrEP regimes are currently recommended in the United States: TDF/FTC, tenofovir alafenamide (TAF/FTC), and long-acting cabotegravir. [63] [64] The choice of PrEP regime should be based on a discussion between the individual and clinician based on the person's risk factor profile, comorbid conditions, preference for oral or injectable medications, barriers to consistent adherence to medications, in addition to the adverse effect profile and cost of the medication if not covered through insurance or pharmaceutical-sponsored programs. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 276
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class known as integrase inhibitors, which are systemic anti‑infective agents used primarily in the treatment of HIV infection. As integrase inhibitors, each drug targets the HIV integrase enzyme, preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and thereby suppressing viral replication. Their inclusion in this class highlights a shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in antiretroviral regimens, making them important options for clinicians managing patients with HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: Enfuvirtid, delavirdin‑mesilát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are antiretroviral agents that may prevent HIV infection, indicating a role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis. Cabotegravir and fostemsavir are antiretroviral drugs that may be used to treat HIV infection, providing therapeutic options for individuals with established disease. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir and raltegravir are classified as members of the Integrase Inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use, reflecting their common mechanism of inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme. HIV infection is associated with the shared use of needles, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction strategies to prevent transmission. Together, these relationships highlight both preventive and treatment modalities within the integrase inhibitor class and emphasize a key behavioral risk factor for HIV acquisition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly linked to the practice of shared needle use, which constitutes a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents can either prevent acquisition of HIV or treat established infection. Among the nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs), abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, dideoxycytidine (also known as didanosine), and tenofovir disoproxil are recognized members of this drug class. Each of these NRTIs—abacavir, delavirdin‑mesylate (a non‑NRTI), emtricitabine, lamivudine, raltegravir, stavudine, and zidovudine—has been shown to have preventive activity against HIV infection when used in pre‑exposure prophylaxis or as part of combination regimens. Integrase inhibitors, another major class of antiretroviral drugs used systemically, include cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir, all of which are classified as members of the integrase inhibitor group. Cabotegravir, in particular, possesses both preventive potential and therapeutic efficacy, being noted for its ability to treat HIV infection. Dolutegravir also demonstrates preventive properties and belongs to the integrase inhibitor class. Overall, the prevention of HIV infection can be achieved through the use of multiple agents across different drug classes—NRTIs such as abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, as well as integrase inhibitors like cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir. For patients already infected, cabotegravir offers a treatment option, while the broader spectrum of NRTIs and integrase inhibitors provides the foundation for effective antiretroviral therapy. Understanding the relationships among transmission risk factors, preventive agents, therapeutic drugs, and their class memberships is essential for comprehensive HIV management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared‑needle use, which constitutes a major route of viral transmission. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents has been identified for both prevention (pre‑exposure prophylaxis) and treatment of HIV infection. Preventive activity has been reported for delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, whereas cabotegravir is highlighted for its therapeutic effect in established infection. These drugs belong to three principal pharmacologic classes. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – delavirdin, rilpivirine, efavirenz, etravirine and nevirapine – bind directly to the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme and inhibit viral replication. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil – require intracellular phosphorylation and act as chain terminators of reverse transcription. Integrase inhibitors – cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir – target the HIV integrase enzyme, blocking integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Understanding the transmission risk posed by shared‑needle use together with the preventive and therapeutic properties of these NNRTIs, NRTIs and integrase inhibitors, and their class‑specific mechanisms, informs comprehensive strategies for HIV prevention, combination antiretroviral therapy, resistance mitigation and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their ability to prevent acquisition of HIV, including the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine, as well as the integrase inhibitors dolutegravir, raltegravir, and cabotegravir. Among these, cabotegravir is also noted for its therapeutic role in treating established HIV infection. Delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine are all members of the class “Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” while dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir belong to the class “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use.” Doravirine carries a specific contraindication with the antimicrobial rifapentine, indicating that co‑administration should be avoided. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how both NNRTIs and integrase inhibitors contribute to HIV prevention strategies—such as pre‑exposure prophylaxis—and, in the case of cabotegravir, to ongoing treatment regimens, underscoring the importance of class‑based drug selection and awareness of drug‑specific contraindications in comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly linked to the practice of shared needle use, which constitutes a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their roles in either preventing acquisition of HIV or treating established infection. Delavirdin, its mesylate salt (delavirdin‑mesilát), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, raltegravir, and rilpivirine are all reported to have preventive activity against HIV infection, reflecting their use in pre‑exposure prophylaxis or post‑exposure settings. Cabotegravir is noted for its therapeutic potential in managing HIV infection, and may also be employed in long‑acting prophylactic regimens. These drugs belong to two principal pharmacologic classes. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are members of the Integrase Inhibitors (anti‑infectives for systematic use) class, which target the viral integrase enzyme to block integration of viral DNA into the host genome. Delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilát, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine are classified as Non‑nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (NNRTIs), which bind to reverse transcriptase at a site distinct from nucleoside analogues and inhibit viral RNA‑dependent DNA synthesis. Understanding the transmission risk associated with shared needle use and the preventive or therapeutic functions of these integrase inhibitors and NNRTIs is essential for comprehensive HIV prevention strategies and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing viral DNA from integrating into the host genome. Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of this class and are employed systemically in the treatment of HIV infection. By targeting the same viral integration step, these drugs provide essential options for combination antiretroviral therapy, contributing to effective viral suppression and improved patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 829
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to společné užívání jehel (shared needle use), a key risk factor for transmission. Several antiretroviral agents have been identified as having preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdin‑mesylát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir. In addition, cabotegravir has demonstrated therapeutic efficacy for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic group “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use,” indicating that they act by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme and are intended for systemic administration. The grouping of these drugs within the same class underscores their shared mechanism of action and their complementary roles in both prophylaxis (delavirdin‑mesylát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir) and treatment (cabotegravir) of HIV, highlighting the importance of combining behavioral risk‑reduction strategies such as avoiding shared needle use with targeted pharmacologic interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1025
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely associated with the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. In patients with HIV infection, the use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated because of potential adverse interactions. Several antiretroviral agents have preventive activity against HIV infection, including delavirdine‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir. In addition to prevention, cabotegravir and fostemsavir are employed as therapeutic agents for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir are all members of the Integrase Inhibitors class of systemic anti‑infectives, reflecting their shared mechanism of targeting the viral integrase enzyme. This network of transmission risk, drug contraindications, preventive agents, and treatment options underscores the importance of integrated management strategies for individuals at risk for or living with HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are associated with the prevention or treatment of HIV infection. Delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir have been reported to have preventive activity against HIV acquisition, and raltegravir is also recognized for its ability to prevent HIV infection. Cabotegravir, in contrast, is used primarily for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir all belong to the class of integrase inhibitors, a group of systematic anti‑infective agents that target the viral integrase enzyme to block viral replication. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate preventive strategies, therapeutic regimens, and avoiding contraindicated drugs for individuals living with or at risk for HIV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1662
retrieved_docs.content: Cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and elvitegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as integrase inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, these agents target the HIV integrase enzyme and are employed in systemic antiretroviral therapy to prevent viral DNA integration into host cells, thereby contributing to the management of HIV infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is closely linked to the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction counseling in prevention strategies. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents demonstrates preventive activity against HIV acquisition, including delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil. Cabotegravir is recognized for therapeutic use in established HIV infection. These drugs are organized into pharmacologic classes that reflect distinct mechanisms of action: non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine and nevirapine, which bind to a specific site on HIV reverse transcriptase; nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) include dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, acting as intracellularly phosphorylated chain terminators; and integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—target viral DNA integration. Notable drug‑drug contraindications are identified within these classes: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, highlighting the need for careful medication reconciliation. Recognizing the relationships among needle‑sharing risk, the preventive and therapeutic roles of specific antiretrovirals, their class‑based mechanisms, and relevant contraindications is essential for constructing safe, effective HIV prophylaxis and treatment regimens and for optimizing patient counseling and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly linked to the practice of shared needle use, which constitutes a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their preventive or therapeutic activity against HIV. Delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, lamivudine, raltegravir, stavudine and zidovudine are all reported to have the potential to prevent HIV infection, while cabotegravir is noted for its ability to treat established HIV infection. These drugs belong to two principal pharmacologic classes. The nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) class includes tenofovir disoproxil, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine and zidovudine. The integrase inhibitor class, used systemically as anti‑infectives, comprises cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir. Understanding the relationships among transmission risk factors, preventive agents, and therapeutic classes is essential for comprehensive HIV prevention strategies and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, a principal route of transmission, highlighting the need for behavioral risk reduction alongside pharmacologic interventions. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents is employed for both pre‑exposure prophylaxis and treatment. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) that prevent HIV replication include lamivudin, emtricitabin, zidovudin, stavudin, tenofovir disoproxil and dideoxycytidine. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesilát, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapin and rilpivirin, all classified within the NNRTI group. Integrase strand‑transfer inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir and cabotegravir—contribute to prevention, and cabotegravir is also indicated for treatment of established HIV infection. An important safety consideration is the contraindicated interaction between pantoprazole and rilpivirin, which mandates avoidance of their co‑administration. Collectively, these relationships delineate the major antiretroviral drug classes, their individual members, and critical drug‑drug interaction information essential for effective and safe HIV management.
